stage.name: Crypto Markets & Tokenomics (High School)
stage.description: Market cap, tokenomics, supply mechanics, Bitcoin halving cycles, reading price action, and evaluating altcoins — intermediate level.

elements.content: 📊 Market Cap & Tokenomics
Why price alone is a misleading number — High School Level
Market Cap = Price × Circulating Supply

Large Cap
> $10B

BTC, ETH, XRP. Most liquid. Institutional money flows here first. Less volatile than small caps.

Mid Cap
$1B – $10B

Established projects with real usage. Higher upside but also sharper drawdowns.

Small Cap
< $1B

Newer projects. Can 10× quickly but can also lose 90% just as fast. Research carefully.

elements.content: 📊 Tokenomics & Supply Mechanics
How supply design shapes long-term value

Tokenomics = the economic design of a coin. Key factors: hard cap supply (Bitcoin: 21M max = deflationary), inflationary emission (new coins minted continuously for validators), burn mechanisms (permanently destroying coins to reduce supply — Ethereum burns fees, XRP burns ~0.0001 XRP per tx), and VC allocation (pre-mined tokens for team/investors — check vesting schedules for dump risk).
XRP has ~57 billion circulating supply — that's why its price looks 'cheap' vs BTC. You're getting a proportional share of a $30B+ market cap. Supply ≠ value.

elements.content: 🔄 Bitcoin Halving & Reading Price Action
The programmed supply shock and what charts are actually telling you
⚡ Bitcoin Halving Cycles (~every 4 years)
Every 210,000 blocks, miner reward is cut in half. Hardcoded in Bitcoin's protocol — no one can change it. Reduces new BTC entering circulation.

2012
50→25 BTC
Peak ~$1K

2016
25→12.5 BTC
Peak ~$20K

2020
12.5→6.25
Peak ~$69K

2024
6.25→3.125
Peak TBD

scenes.title: Bitcoin Halving & Reading Price Action (Part B)

elements.content: 📈 Reading Price Action & Evaluating Altcoins
What charts are telling you and how to assess new projects
📈 Reading Basic Price Action

Uptrend (Bullish): Higher highs + higher lows. Each rally peak exceeds the last. Each pullback holds above the previous dip. Price stays above 50-day and 200-day moving averages.

Downtrend (Bearish): Lower highs + lower lows. Each rally fails below the previous peak. Each drop breaks the prior support level. Price below moving averages.

⚠️ Halving caveat: Past cycles don't guarantee future performance. With each cycle, more capital is needed to move the market and institutional participants increasingly shape price action. The pattern may weaken over time.

Evaluating Altcoins: What problem does it solve (in one sentence)? Who's building it — are they public? Does the token actually need to exist to use the network? Check on-chain metrics — active wallets, transaction volume, GitHub activity. Real usage = real demand. Dead chains fake these metrics.

elements.content: 🐳 Whales & Market Manipulation
How large holders move markets — and what to watch for

A whale is any wallet holding a large enough position to move the market when they buy or sell. In Bitcoin, a wallet with 1,000+ BTC qualifies. In smaller altcoins, even $500k can move the price.


Common manipulation tactics: wash trading (buying and selling to yourself to fake volume), pump and dump (coordinated hype then mass sell), spoofing (placing and canceling large orders to fake pressure).

🐳 Whale
Holds enough to move price

🔄 Wash Trade
Fake volume — self-trading

📣 Pump & Dump
Hype it up, then sell on retail

📊 On-Chain Tools
Track whale wallets publicly

elements.content: 📖 Reading an Order Book
The real-time record of all buy and sell intentions

An order book shows all active buy orders (bids) and sell orders (asks) for an asset. The gap between the highest bid and lowest ask is the spread.


Large clusters of buy orders at a price level = support (price tends to bounce there). Large clusters of sell orders = resistance (price tends to stall there). Watching the order book gives you a live pulse of market intent.

📗 Bids
Buy orders — below current price

📕 Asks
Sell orders — above current price

↔️ Spread
Gap between best bid and ask

💡 Depth
Wall of orders = support or resistance

actions.text: 📖 Reading an Order Book
The real-time record of all buy and sell intentions

An order book shows all active buy orders (bids) and sell orders (asks) for an asset. The gap between the highest bid and lowest ask is the spread. Large clusters of buy orders at a price level = support (price tends to bounce there). Large clusters of sell orders = resistance (price tends to stall there). Watching the order book gives you a live pulse of market intent
📗 Bids. Buy orders — below current price
📕 Asks. Sell orders — above current price
↔️ Spread. Gap between best bid and ask
💡 Depth. Wall of orders = support or resistance

elements.content: 💧 Liquidity & Slippage
Why market depth matters when you trade

Liquidity is how easily you can buy or sell without moving the price. Bitcoin is highly liquid — you can trade millions of dollars without much price impact. A small altcoin is illiquid — even a $10k buy can spike the price 5%.


Slippage is when your order executes at a worse price than expected because there weren't enough orders at your target price. Always use limit orders when trading illiquid assets.

💧 High Liquidity
Large trades, small price impact

🏜️ Low Liquidity
Small trades move price a lot

📉 Slippage
Got a worse price than expected

✅ Fix
Use limit orders, not market orders

content.html:
📊 Market Cap Calculator

Market Cap = Price × Circulating Supply. Design your own token and see how it compares to real coins.

actions.text: Let's explore this interactively. Use the simulation below to experiment with the concepts we just covered.
Try different inputs and see how the outputs change. Hands-on exploration builds intuition faster than reading.

scenes.title: Knowledge Check — Crypto Markets High School

questions.question: A coin is priced at $0.001 with 1 trillion tokens in supply. What is its market cap?

options.label: $0.001 million
$1 billion
$1 million
$1 trillion

questions.analysis: Market cap = price × supply = $0.001 × 1,000,000,000,000 = $1,000,000,000 = $1 billion. This is why a low price doesn't mean a coin is cheap — it's the market cap that shows the real valuation.

questions.question: What happens to Bitcoin's block reward during a halving?

options.label: It doubles
It is cut in half
It resets to the original 50 BTC
It becomes variable based on demand

questions.analysis: Every ~4 years (210,000 blocks), the Bitcoin block reward is cut in half. This is hardcoded into Bitcoin's protocol — no one can change it. It reduces the rate of new BTC entering circulation, historically creating deflationary pressure.

questions.question: What does 'higher highs and higher lows' on a price chart indicate?

options.label: A downtrend
A sideways market
An uptrend — buyers are in control
A price correction

questions.analysis: Higher highs (each rally peak exceeds the last) and higher lows (each pullback holds above the previous dip) are the definition of an uptrend. Buyers are consistently stepping in at higher prices — a bullish structure.

questions.question: Why does XRP have a lower price than Bitcoin despite being a top-10 cryptocurrency?

options.label: XRP is less valuable as a technology
XRP has a far larger circulating supply (~57 billion vs ~21 million for BTC)
XRP is not as widely traded
XRP has no real-world use cases

questions.analysis: XRP has approximately 57 billion tokens in circulation versus Bitcoin's ~21 million. A larger supply means the same market cap is spread across more tokens, making the per-token price lower. XRP's market cap places it in the top 5–10 globally — it's not 'cheap.'

questions.question: What is a 'burn mechanism' in tokenomics?

options.label: Miners burning energy to create blocks
Permanently destroying tokens to reduce circulating supply
Locking tokens in a smart contract temporarily
Converting tokens into stablecoins

questions.analysis: A burn mechanism permanently removes tokens from circulation — they're sent to an unspendable address and can never be accessed. Ethereum burns a portion of every transaction fee (EIP-1559). XRP burns a tiny fee per transaction. Over time, burns reduce supply, which can create upward price pressure if demand stays constant.

questions.question: What causes a 'Death Cross' in technical analysis?

options.label: RSI drops below 30
The 50-day MA crosses below the 200-day MA
Volume drops to zero for 24 hours
The MACD line goes negative

questions.analysis: A Death Cross occurs when the shorter-term 50-day moving average crosses below the longer-term 200-day MA — historically a bearish signal. The opposite (50 crossing above 200) is the Golden Cross.

questions.question: What is 'wash trading'?

options.label: Swapping tokens on a DEX to clean your wallet history
Buying and selling to yourself to create fake trading volume
Transferring tokens between exchanges to avoid detection
Staking tokens to increase liquidity

questions.analysis: Wash trading is market manipulation where a trader simultaneously buys and sells the same asset to inflate reported volume. It's used to create the illusion of market interest.

actions.text: Time to test your understanding of crypto markets and tokenomics. Five questions covering market cap, halving cycles, price action, and token supply mechanics.
Take your time. Understanding these concepts helps you evaluate any crypto project more clearly.
